meta.title: C215 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes C215 engine — M113 V8, M137 V12, M275 twin-turbo V12. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the M137 warning. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes S-Class C215 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C215 engine typically costs £3,500–£13,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M113 V8 sits at £3,500–£9,000, the M137 V12 at £5,500–£11,000, and the M275 twin-turbo V12 at £6,000–£13,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on engine complexity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes S-Class C215 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M113.960 V8 in the CL 500 is our top recommendation — it's widely regarded as one of the most reliable modern Mercedes engines ever built. The naturally aspirated 5.0-litre V8 has a proven reputation for exceeding 200,000 miles with routine maintenance. The CL 55 AMG shares the same core reliability.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes S-Class C215?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler O-ring failure on the M137 V12 (pre-facelift CL 600/CL 63 AMG), thermostat and MAF sensor failure on the M113 V8 (CL 500/CL 55 AMG), supercharger clutch and intercooler pump failure on the M113K (CL 55 AMG Kompressor), and coil pack and turbo actuator failure on the M275 twin-turbo V12 (post-facelift CL 600/CL 65 AMG).

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes S-Class C215 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The M113 V8 is genuinely reliable and well-proven. The M113K supercharged V8 adds complexity but is still solid. The M275 twin-turbo V12 is reliable for what it is but expensive to maintain. The M137 V12 is the generation's biggest risk — oil cooler O-ring failure is common and costly.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes S-Class C215 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M137 V12 (pre-facelift CL 600/CL 63 AMG) is the one we consistently advise against unless you have a serious budget for maintenance — oil cooler O-ring failures are common and labour-intensive to repair. A neglected M275 V12 without coil pack history is also a risk. The M113 V8 is the safe choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C215 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil leaks from the V of the engine (M137 oil cooler O-rings), poor idle or cold running (M113 thermostat/MAF), loss of boost or supercharger noise (M113K), or misfires (M275 coil packs) are the key warning signs. Check engine lights on any variant should be diagnosed immediately.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes S-Class C215 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000 CL 500, a £3,500–£6,000 M113 recon is 45–75% — borderline. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon exceeds value — walk away. For the V12, against a £10,000+ CL 600, a £6,000–£10,000 recon is 60–100% — viable only on exceptional examples. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line: value the car first because the engine is often worth more than the car.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes S-Class C215 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M113 V8 is proven to exceed 200,000 miles with diligent maintenance — thermostat and MAF sensors are the main wear items. The M275 V12 can reach 150,000+ miles with proper servicing — coil packs are the main cost. The M137 V12 is the shortest-lived; many need significant work before 100,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C215 and the W220?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both share the same platform and most engines. The C215 (1998–2006, this page) is the 2-door, 4-seat coupé (CL-Class), while the W220 (1998–2005) is the 4-door, 5-seat saloon (S-Class). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see the W220 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes S-Class C215 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (e.g., CL 500) and approximate registration year (pre- or post-2002 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes S-Class C215 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second-generation CL-Class coupé — from the bulletproof M113 V8 to the thunderous M275 twin-turbo V12 and the rare 6.3-litre M137. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the pre- and post-facelift engine changes that defined this flagship. Backed by 880+ real UK M113-related enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes S-Class C215 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C215 coupé — including the 2002 facelift that brought the M275 twin-turbo V12 and the M113K supercharged V8.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: M113: 90% confidence — widely regarded as one of the most reliable modern Mercedes engines; well-documented failure patterns. M275: 85% confidence — robust twin-turbo V12 with established pattern data. M137: 70% confidence — known for oil cooler O-ring failures and considered one of Mercedes' less reliable engines.

overview.intro: The C215 is the second generation of the Mercedes-Benz CL-Class, introduced in 1998 as the flagship two-door, four-passenger coupé. Based on the W220 S-Class but with an 8-inch shorter wheelbase, it featured extensive use of aluminium, magnesium and composite materials for a 340 kg weight reduction over its predecessor. A 2002 facelift brought revised styling and a new engine line-up, including the M113K supercharged V8 and the M275 twin-turbo V12.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz W220 (S-Class) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz CL-Class C140 (W140-based) • Successor: Mercedes-Benz CL-Class C216 • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (C215 is a standalone coupé; shares platform with W220 S-Class saloon).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The M113 V8 engine family remains one of the most-requested Mercedes engines in our data, with enquiries concentrated on the CL 500 and CL 55 AMG as these grand tourers enter classic-car territory.

items.quote: no diesel engine fitted to the C215.

The 5.0-litre M113 V8 is arguably the most reliable modern Mercedes engine ever built — naturally aspirated, simple SOHC design, and proven to exceed 200,000 miles with routine maintenance. Thermostat and MAF sensor issues are its only real demands.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want bulletproof V8 reliability in a flagship coupé.

items.quote: The M137 V12 is possibly the least reliable modern Mercedes engine — oil cooler O-ring failures are a £1,000+ repair, and the complex 12-cylinder architecture makes it a specialist-only proposition. Mercedes replaced it with the M275 for good reason.
items.whoItsFor: collectors who accept the risks of a rare V12 flagship.

items.quote: The 612 bhp twin-turbo V12 delivers tyre-shredding torque and a soundtrack to match, but it's an ageing AMG flagship — turbocharger wear, coil pack failure and the sheer cost of V12 parts make it the most expensive engine in the range to maintain.
items.whoItsFor: AMG collectors with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: The entry-level V8 offers genuine grand-touring performance (6.1s to 60mph) with significantly lower running costs than any V12 — and sits in the same used price bracket as lesser cars. It's the sweet spot of the range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the C215 experience without the V12's running costs.

items.quote: The supercharged 5.4-litre V8 is often overshadowed by the V12s, but it offers genuine AMG performance (4.7s to 60mph) with none of the M137's reliability issues — and the supercharger whine is addictive.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG performance without the V12's complexity.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best V8 — reliable, proven, great value.
Solid AMG — more power, same reliability.
Supercharged AMG — more power, more complexity.
Flagship AMG — ultimate performance, ultimate cost.

economicsRule.title: The C215 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500–£6,000 M113 recon against a £5,000–£15,000 C215 sits at 25–120% of the car's value — on sub-£6,000 examples, it often approaches or exceeds our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. The M275 V12 recon at £6,000–£13,000 against a £8,000–£25,000 CL 600/65 is 25–160% — viable only on well-maintained, higher-value examples. These are ageing flagship coupés; value the car first, because the engine can easily be worth more than the car on budget examples.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C215 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CL 500 (M113) — cheapest recon, best reliability, simplest maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C215 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CL 65 AMG — V12 twin-turbo parts and specialist labour costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C215 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: CL 500 — V8 grand touring without the V12's running costs.

problems.title: M137 Oil Cooler O-Ring Failure
problems.affectedModels: C215 CL 600, CL 63 AMG (pre-facelift, M137 V12 variants).
problems.rootCause: The M137 V12's oil cooler O-rings degrade with age and heat cycling, causing oil leaks. The repair requires significant disassembly of the V12 engine bay, making labour costs substantial.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil leaks on any M137-powered C215, have the oil cooler O-rings inspected — an £800–£1,500 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to engine failure.

cta.label: Compare M137 engine prices for your C215 CL 600 →

problems.title: M113 Thermostat & MAF Sensor Failure
problems.affectedModels: C215 CL 500, CL 55 AMG (all M113 variants).
problems.rootCause: The map-controlled thermostat and mass airflow (MAF) sensor are the two most common failures on the M113 V8 — thermostat failure causes the engine to run cold, while MAF sensor failure causes poor idle and reduced power.
problems.recommendation: If your CL 500 or CL 55 AMG runs cold or has a poor idle, have the thermostat and MAF sensor inspected — a £300–£800 repair is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows if lean-running causes engine damage.

cta.label: Compare M113 engine prices for your C215 CL 500 →

problems.title: M113K Supercharger Clutch & Intercooler Pump Failure
problems.affectedModels: C215 CL 55 AMG Kompressor (post-facelift, M113.991 variants).
problems.rootCause: The supercharger clutch engages and disengages under load — wear leads to clutch failure, loss of boost, and in some cases, supercharger seizure. The intercooler pump is also a known weak point, with failure leading to heat soak and reduced power.
problems.recommendation: If your CL 55 AMG Kompressor loses boost or makes unusual supercharger noises, have the clutch and intercooler pump inspected — a £500–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows if the supercharger fails catastrophically.

cta.label: Compare M113K engine prices for your C215 CL 55 AMG Kompressor →

problems.title: M275 V12 Coil Pack & Turbo Actuator Failure
problems.affectedModels: C215 CL 600, CL 65 AMG (post-facelift, M275 twin-turbo variants).
problems.rootCause: The M275's twin-turbo V12 uses 24 spark plugs and 12 individual coil packs — coil pack failure is common and causes misfires. Turbocharger actuators can also seize, causing loss of boost and reduced power.
problems.recommendation: If your CL 600 or CL 65 AMG develops a misfire, have the coil packs checked immediately — a £600–£1,500 coil pack replacement is far cheaper than the £7,000+ replacement that follows if misfire damages the catalytic converters.

cta.label: Compare M275 engine prices for your C215 CL 600 →

replacementCosts.h2: C215 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the C215 shares its W220 platform with the S-Class saloon, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are C215 CL-Class coupé only (1998–2006). The W220 S-Class saloon (1998–2005) shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page. Note the pre-facelift (1998–2002) and post-facelift (2002–2006) engine line-up differences: pre-facelift cars use M113 V8s and M137 V12s; post-facelift cars use M113K supercharged V8s and M275 twin-turbo V12s.

mostCommonFailures: 3. M275 coil pack & turbo actuator failure

liveFeed.issue: Oil leak from engine bay / oil consumption
